---
title: "Lab #7: Components of AI (30 pts)"
author: "PUT NAME HERE"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
execute: 
  echo: true
knitr:
  opts_chunk: 
    message: false
editor: source
---

## Introduction

As you've seen in this week's lectures, much forethought has to go into the creation of a model for artificial intelligence, whether it is from the business, engineering, or ethical side of things. We will focus on the engineering and ethical portions by using a poorly-made example of a natural language processor using Markov chains.

Before we dive into the mechanics of the model, discuss with others around you and write answers regarding the following questions:

::: {.callout-important}
###### Q1 (3 pts)

Think about the following sentence: "I am having a great day!". What rules or considerations do I need to follow linguistically in order to convey that sentiment? Is there an order to how those words are being said?

#I need to consider that I am the one having a great day and start analysing why I would say that statement to express a good day. I could attribute the feeling to weather but since the weather is not good I would find another reason to attribute it to. Yes, there is an order because if words were swapped the statement wont makes sense linguistically.

:::

::: {.callout-important}
###### Q2 (3 pts)

Think about the following sentence: "I am having a great day!". Are there replacements I can make for each word that would still result in a comprehensible sentence?

#Yes, I can replace all the words and still create a comprehensible statement as far as there are no limits to the type of replacements that can be done for example "She was leaving her mom's place".

:::

::: {.callout-important}
###### Q3 (3 pts)

Think about the following sentence: "I am having a great day!". How often am I using each of these words in a sentence? Does it depend on the situation?

#Yes it depends on the situation because feelings change overtime with different situations including weather and activities.

:::

## Behind the AI

Despite over-analyzing the six-worded sentence, it is important to understand the mechanics of this sentence generation when it comes to generating responses. A large-language model (LLM) is a computational model that uses a bunch of data for natural language processing. When we build a model, we want to consider the appropriate methods that can be used for training and results, as well the inputs and outputs associated with our method.

Pivoting back to our discussion on how sentences are created, one model we might create builds upon the idea of predicting what the next word might be given the word before, based on the part of speech and subject. Here, we venture into using [Markov chains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Markov_chain). 

We will need to assign probabilities (and/or weights) to how the next word of a sentence comes about. Additionally, we'll need a dictionary of words to start with.

There are many sophisticated models that can be used for natural language processing, including but not limited to:

- Deep learning models
- Neural networks
- Large-language models

We will not delve into these specific models; rather, we will focus on a more simplistic model to garner a better understanding of how a probabilistic model can fuel generation of a sentence.

I have created two models to aid in our creation of sentences:

- A model to generate probabilities of certain words being used, given their part of speech (i.e. noun, adjective)
- A model to generate probabilities of certain parts of speech being used next given the part of speech from the previous word

These two models allow for the determination of a word given what part of speech we're using and what the next word might be given what word we've used. As you'll see in a second, this approach is rather interesting.

To incorporate these two models and generate sentences, we will use two different datasets:

- `wordData.csv`: This contains 114 different words with the part of speech they fall under, their usage probability, and the chance of which part of speech comes next.
  - Words come from the list of most commonly used words in English (Source: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Most_common_words_in_English))
- `tranMatData.csv`: This is a transition matrix that allows us to go from one word to the next.

A transition matrix can be thought of as a grid of outcomes/chances depending on the action. A small example is to determine what mood you'll be in the next day given how you feel currently. If you're in a bad mood today, perhaps there's a 60\% chance you'll be in a good mood tomorrow. On the flip side, if you're in a good mood today, perhaps there will be a 45\% chance you'll be in a good mood tomorrow. When placed in a transition matrix, we get the following:

|        | Happy | Sad  |
|:-------|:------|:-----|
| Happy  | 0.45  | 0.55 |
| Sad    | 0.60  | 0.40 |

In our example, we are using the transition matrix to go from one word to the next. Load in the [wordData.csv](wordData.csv) and [tranMatData.csv](tranMatData.csv) files and then take a look at the `head()` of the two data files.

```{r}
wordData <- read.csv("wordData.csv")
tranMatData <- read.csv("tranMatData.csv")

head(wordData)
head(tranMatData)
```

Answer the following questions below:

::: {.callout-important}
###### Q4 (3 pts)

Look at `wordData`. The `prob` column provides the probability of that word being used given what its part of speech is. Determine if these probabilities are legitimate for each of these words. What other considerations do I need to make when determining the probability?

#I think the probabilities of using the words are wrong because the probabilities of a word being used change depending on the context and the word used before it.

:::

::: {.callout-important}
###### Q5 (3 pts)

Look at `tranMatData`. Each row provides the probability that the corresponding word will be followed by the word specified by the column. Determine if these probabilities are legitimate for each of these words. What other considerations do I need to make when determining the probability?

#I think the probabilities of the words might be correct because this table gives its probabilities depending on the present word and context.

:::

::: {.callout-important}
###### Q6 (3 pts)

Based on both of the datasets, what are some issues that may arise when using this data?

#Some issues that may arise is the datasets do not have all words in the dictionary and may repeat words or cannot produce others when necessary.

:::

## Running the Model

Now onto the fun part -- we are going to create our own sentences! To do so, run the following chunk below:

```{r}
# Code to provide the first word of the sentence
sentence = c("the")
wordInd = which(wordData$word == "the")

# We can specify the number of words we want in our sentence here
numWords = 6

# Run this code to create the sentence
for (i in 1:(numWords - 1)){
  wordInd = sample(1:nrow(wordData), size = 1, prob = tranMatData[wordInd, ])
  sentence = c(sentence, wordData$word[wordInd])
}

# Print the sentence here
paste(sentence, collapse = " ")
```

There you have it -- we've generated sentences! But as you see, the results are interesting to say the least. You'll notice that every time you run it, you will generate a new sentence. Some may make sense, while others may not. 

As you've probably discussed in previous questions, there are many, many considerations to generating these sentences. The following questions will bring the discussion back full-circle:


::: {.callout-important}
###### Q7 (3 pts)

Run the code above 5 different times using a `numWords` of 6. Copy and paste the results below.
#"the time have of time be"
#"the world do to year see"
#"the time up great way say"
#"the man see fact say time"
#"the way use place a life"
#"the time do way know day"
:::

::: {.callout-important}
###### Q8 (3 pts)

What are some observations you notice from the sentences? Are there any similarities? Differences? Confusions? Do the sentences make sense?

#None of the sentences make sense. The similarities I see is that they all start with "the" and the sentences can be grouped as some have more words in common and others do not.A major difference is in the arrangements of words in the sentence even though some sentences have words in same position. I am confused about what is considered for wording because even though all sentences start with "the", the words that follow vary.

:::

::: {.callout-important}
###### Q9 (3 pts)

What are some factors we may want to factor into our program aside from:

- the probability of a word being used, and
- what the next word will be given the part of speech of the previous word?

#we may factor what next word will be given depending on all previous words.
#we may factor, word probability depending on context.

:::

::: {.callout-important}
###### Q10 (3 pts)

Suppose we attempted to use this model in lieu of more sophisticated programs like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude. What would your perception be? How would this lead to issues with resolving prompts you fed to the programs?

#my perception would be that the models are dumb and do not understand my questions inorder to help me.I may not even bother to resolve prompts because I will feel discouraged to ask a question and get answers that are not comprehensible and related.


:::

## Wrapup

Thankfully, these sophisticated programs are able to answer questions and provide responses that answer our questions, do so in a natural way, and give additional information aside from the initial ask. This is what has made these programs so intriguing and highly used over the last few years.

However, as you've seen with this example, developing these models takes a lot of foresight and power. The factors you've discussed in this activity only scratch the surface of all potential variables and factors to consider when developing a model. Additionally, we only looked at prompt generation and not into other avenues, such as comprehending prompts. 

If you've already exhausted a lot of brain power thinking about these factors, imagine the power it takes to factor these considerations into a series of models and executing the results. The data itself will also compound these actions. Also, as you've seen here, artificial intelligence relies on the success of the models, data, and processes behind the scenes. Nothing is perfect, and at times, you may find that these models will produce odd, incomplete, or unrelated answers.

## Prompt

In Slack (under the `#discussions` channel), provide an example of a time you used an large language model like ChatGPT to resolve a prompt and it generated a rather unusual response. Consider why the response was generated as such.
#In high school, when I just started using ChatGPT, many said it could help with chemical equations, so I wanted to predict the products of an equation. I had an idea of the product, but I just wanted assurance and to see how authentic ChatGPT was.When I typed the products, it gave me a totally wrong answer, and I figured that because I had an idea of the right answer. So, I wrote a prompt saying it was wrong and gave an idea of my answer and why it should be my answer. It then responded, saying my answer was right. This was hilarious. Since then, I have had the notion that ChatGPT is sometimes wrong and right, and is a student like me who sometimes forgets answers. After doing this exercise, I better understand that ChatGPT doesn't think and just calculates, and maybe a wrong answer was generated because it did not have words in its dictionary for my products.
